turns.content: I've read about Bevy's Query system and how you can filter components like Transform or Velocity, but I still don't fully understand the edge case with Changed and Added filters when iterating over queries. Can you help me clarify this?

Think of Queries as a library where you can ask for specific books, but sometimes you want to know which ones were added or changed recently. The Changed and Added filters are like timestamps on those books; they tell you if the book's content has been updated since your last scan. When iterating over a query with these filters, Bevy ensures each entity is only processed once per frame for changes, preventing duplicate work.
That makes sense in theory, but what happens if I try to use Changed<(&Transform)> in combination with a filter that includes Velocity? Does Bevy handle the component mismatch gracefully?

Bevy enforces strict type safety here. If you query for Changed<(&Transform)>, it only returns entities where Transform exists and has changed. Adding a filter like Velocity requires that entity to also have a Velocity component, so the final iteration will yield only those entities that satisfy both conditions. Bevy automatically checks component existence before applying filters.
So if an entity has both Transform and Velocity but neither changed this frame, it won't appear in the Changed<(&Transform)> query even though I want to check its Velocity separately?

Exactly. The Changed filter only yields entities where at least one component in the tuple has been modified since the last frame. If you need to inspect Velocity regardless of whether it changed, you must use a separate query without the Changed or Added filters. Bevy doesn't combine unrelated component changes into a single iteration unless explicitly requested via compound queries.

Ah, so I need two separate iterations—one for Changed<(&Transform)> and another for all entities with Velocity? That seems inefficient compared to my old Unity approach where I could batch everything in one loop?

You're right that this feels different from Unity's batching, but Bevy's design prioritizes explicitness and correctness over raw iteration speed. In Rust, you can optimize the two queries into a single pass if your logic allows by checking component existence first, then applying filters conditionally. This avoids unnecessary allocations and ensures type safety without runtime overhead.
I'm trying to run this tutorial but I keep getting an error that says no type was found for the query system. What went wrong?

That usually happens when you try to use a Query without first defining it in your main function using the query macro or builder pattern. In Bevy, you must declare the specific entity type you want to filter before running any iteration logic. For example, if you need entities with both position and velocity data, you should define something like Query<(&Transform, &Velocity)>; as a variable named 'my_query' in your main function so the engine knows what to search for.
Okay, I added that query declaration but now my code compiles but nothing happens when the game runs. It seems like it's ignoring entities that have both components attached.

The issue is likely with how you are filtering those specific entity types together. When you combine multiple components in a single Query, you must apply filters to ensure the system only processes entities that actually possess all the required traits at once. For instance, if you want to track objects moving through space, you should use filters like Changed<Velocity> or Added<Velocity> alongside your base query so Bevy knows which entities satisfy every condition before executing the loop.
I'm trying to get the velocity of all entities that have a Transform component, but I keep getting stuck on how to write this query.

You can use a standard bundle query like `Query<(Entity, &Transform, &Velocity)>` if you want the entity ID along with the components. However, for just the velocities without the IDs, you'd use `Query<&Velocity>` and then filter it down to only those that also have Transform.

Wait, I thought queries always required Entity? That sounds wrong because my system doesn't need to know which entity is moving, just the velocity itself. Also, can I run this with or without filters?

You are correct that you don't always need the Entity in your query tuple. For a pure bundle like `Query<&Velocity>`, it will iterate over all entities in the world, and you can apply an `Or` filter to restrict it further if needed.
Okay, but what happens if I just run `Query<&Velocity>` without any filters? Will it actually skip entities that don't have Velocity?

Yes, the query system automatically skips entities missing required components. If you filter for `(Entity, &Transform, &Velocity)` but an entity lacks either Transform or Velocity, it simply won't appear in the iteration results.
I see, so I can just use `Query<&Velocity>` and iterate over it directly. But what if I later want to add a filter for entities that have changed since the last frame?

You can add an `Added<T>` or `Changed<T>` filter using the `Or` combinator, like `Or::all([Added<Velocity>, Changed<Transform>])`. This lets you target entities that either just received a Velocity component or have had their Transform modified.
That makes sense. So I can combine filters dynamically in my system setup without hardcoding everything upfront?
Exactly, Bevy's filter builder allows you to construct complex conditions like that at runtime using the `Or` and `And` combinators. You just pass them into your query definition.
